steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Predict Write Result Evidence · Theory Write Result Evidence
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory TypeScript file-delivery team handles case 1924 during a browser upload compatibility rollout. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to verify PutObject bytes, checksum, metadata, and encryption without ETag overclaim; do not treat TypeScript types, a resolved promise, CORS, ETag, or an emulator as stronger proof than it is; then replace one typed fixture with unknown runtime input and prove rejection before AWS use. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Competency probe: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, record bucket, key, version, checksum, size, request ID, encryption, policy decision, and changed-read verification without logging secrets.

For S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Defend the Write Result Evidence model · Theory Write Result Evidence, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, file, object, policy, transfer, integrity, cache, fault, cost, restore, and controlled AWS transfer evidence: The object key is enough evidence to audit a write. Type annotations, generated SDK shapes, and resolved promises do not strengthen that runtime claim.
